walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Game Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Raiders of the North Sea set up! Place the game board in the center of the table where everyone can see it. The board is divided into two main areas. On the left side is the village, where you will hire crew and gather resources. On the right side are the raid locations — harbours, outposts, monasteries, and fortresses — where you will send your Vikings to plunder.

walkthrough.title: Place the Black Worker
walkthrough.text: Find the single black worker piece. This worker is shared by all players and starts on a specific village space as indicated in the rulebook. The black worker is special — you need it to initiate raids. Place it on its starting space now.

walkthrough.title: Fill the Offering Bag
walkthrough.text: Gather all eighteen offering tiles and drop them into the cloth bag. Give the bag a good shake to mix them up. Offerings are earned from raids and scored as sets at the end of the game. The more diverse your offerings, the more points they are worth.

walkthrough.title: Place Valkyrie Tokens
walkthrough.text: Find the seven Valkyrie tokens and set them near the Valkyrie track on the board. When your crew members die during raids, you advance on this track. Higher positions score significant victory points at the end of the game. Yes, you want your Vikings to die gloriously in battle.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Plunder Tiles
walkthrough.text: Place plunder tiles face down on each raid location as indicated on the board. Each location shows where tiles go. Plunder is your primary reward for raiding — each tile is worth victory points. You will not know the exact value until you complete the raid and flip the tile.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Resource Supply
walkthrough.text: Sort the resource tokens into separate piles within reach of all players. You need piles of silver, gold, iron, provisions, and livestock. Silver is currency for hiring crew. Gold is worth victory points at the end. Iron, provisions, and livestock are spent to meet raid requirements.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle the Townsfolk Deck
walkthrough.text: Shuffle all thirty-two townsfolk cards into a face-down draw pile. These are your potential crew members, each with a hiring cost, crew strength, and special ability. Reveal a row of available crew cards next to the draw pile — the exact number depends on your player count.

walkthrough.title: Give Each Player a White Worker
walkthrough.text: Each player takes one white worker piece. This is your personal worker that you will place on village spaces each turn. You always start your turn holding one worker. Keep it ready in your hand.

walkthrough.title: Place Score Markers
walkthrough.text: Each player takes a score marker in their chosen color and places it on zero on the victory point track running around the edge of the board. You will advance this marker whenever you score points during the game.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Crew and Resources
walkthrough.text: Deal starting townsfolk cards to each player as indicated in the rulebook. The number varies by player count. Each player also receives a small amount of starting silver, with later players in turn order receiving slightly more to compensate for going second or third.

walkthrough.title: Determine First Player
walkthrough.text: Choose a starting player randomly. Play proceeds clockwise from that person. Remember, later players get a bit more starting silver to balance out the first-player advantage. You are ready to raid! Let's learn how to play.

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: Raiders of the North Sea is a worker placement game where you play Viking raiders. You hire crew, gather resources in the village, then raid settlements along the coast. The twist is a unique place-one-take-one mechanic — every turn you place a worker and pick up a different one, always performing two actions. The game has no fixed rounds. Players take turns until an endgame condition triggers.

walkthrough.title: The Two-Step Turn
walkthrough.text: Every turn follows the same two-step structure. Step one: take the worker from your hand and place it on an empty space on the board, then perform that space's action. Step two: pick up a different worker from any occupied space, then perform that space's action too. You always do exactly two actions per turn — one from placing, one from picking up.

walkthrough.title: Worker Colors Matter
walkthrough.text: There are two colors of workers, and they matter. White workers are used in the village on the left side of the board. The black worker is required to initiate raids on the right side. On a typical village turn, you place your white worker on a village space, do the action, then pick up the black worker from wherever it sits, doing that action too. To raid, you place the black worker on a raid location and pick up a white worker from the village.

walkthrough.title: Village Actions: Gathering Resources
walkthrough.text: The Silversmith gives you silver. The Armoury gives you iron. The Provisions space gives you provisions. The Treasury gives you gold, which is worth victory points at game end. Each space does exactly what it says — place a worker, take the resource.

walkthrough.title: Village Actions: Hiring Crew
walkthrough.text: The Barracks and Town Hall let you hire crew members from the available row. Each townsfolk card shows a hiring cost in silver, a crew strength value, and a special ability. Pay the silver cost to take the card into your hand. Some crew have military icons (sword) for fortress raids. Some have spiritual icons (cross) for monastery raids. Building the right crew is essential for raiding.

walkthrough.title: Village Actions: Drawing Cards
walkthrough.text: The Long House lets you draw townsfolk cards from the deck. This gives you more options when hiring. The Gate House provides provisions and helps prepare for upcoming raids. Use these spaces when you need more crew options or resources.

walkthrough.title: How Raiding Works
walkthrough.text: To raid, you must place the black worker on a raid location. You need to meet two requirements. First, you must have enough crew cards of the right type — the raid shows how many crew and what type are needed. Second, you must pay the required resources — provisions, iron, or livestock depending on the location. Discard the required crew cards and resources, then collect your rewards.

walkthrough.title: Raid Difficulty Tiers
walkthrough.text: Raid locations are arranged by difficulty from top to bottom on the board. Harbours are the easiest — they need fewer crew and fewer resources. Outposts are medium difficulty. Monasteries are hard and require crew with spiritual icons. Fortresses are the hardest, requiring the largest crews, iron, and often military crew. Harder raids give better rewards.

walkthrough.title: Raid Rewards
walkthrough.text: When you complete a raid, you receive several rewards. You collect the plunder tile from that location, which is worth victory points. You draw offering tiles from the bag. And if any of your crew cards have Valkyrie symbols on them, those crew die in battle and you advance on the Valkyrie track. All of these contribute to your final score.

walkthrough.title: The Valkyrie Track
walkthrough.text: This is a major scoring element that rewards sending crew to die in raids. When crew with Valkyrie icons are discarded during a raid, you advance your marker on the track. Higher positions score more victory points at game end — up to sixteen points for the top position. Some crew are designed to be expendable, with Valkyrie icons but low hiring costs.

walkthrough.title: Offerings and Set Collection
walkthrough.text: After raids, you draw offering tiles from the bag. Offerings come in different types, and you score victory points for collecting sets of different types at game end. The scoring is exponential — a set of one is worth little, but a set of five different types is worth significant points. Diversity in your offerings is very valuable.

walkthrough.title: Armour Scoring
walkthrough.text: Certain plunder tiles and crew cards provide armour icons. At the end of the game, you score victory points based on your total armour collected. Armour is a steady bonus on top of your other scoring — every icon counts.

walkthrough.title: Gold Scoring
walkthrough.text: Gold tokens are gained from the Treasury action and sometimes as raid rewards. Each gold token is worth one victory point at game end. Gold is a safe, predictable source of points, but raiding typically provides more total scoring potential per action.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Triggers
walkthrough.text: The game ends when one of these conditions is met: all offering tiles in the supply are taken, enough Valkyrie tokens have been claimed, or enough raid locations are completed. When triggered, finish the current round so all players have equal turns. Then proceed to final scoring.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: Add up victory points from all sources: plunder tiles, Valkyrie track position, offering sets, gold tokens, armour icons, and any crew card bonuses. The player with the most total victory points wins. If tied, the player with more remaining silver wins the tiebreaker.

summary.title: Game Overview
bullets: Viking worker placement with place-one-take-one mechanic
Hire crew, gather resources, raid coastal settlements
No fixed rounds — play until endgame triggers

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the North Sea
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted opening with three example players. Each player will take different approaches — one focuses on crew building, one rushes toward raiding, and one makes some common mistakes along the way. Follow along to learn the place-one-take-one rhythm.

walkthrough.title: Starting Position
walkthrough.text: All three players have their white workers in hand. The black worker sits on its starting space in the village. Each player has a few starting crew cards and some silver. The board shows village spaces on the left and tempting raid locations on the right. Let's start building our war parties.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Gathering Silver
walkthrough.text: Then they pick up the black worker from its starting space, performing that space's action as well. Player one now holds the black worker and has silver to hire crew. Two actions in one turn — that is the rhythm.

walkthrough.title: The Place-One-Take-One Rhythm
walkthrough.text: Every turn is the same: place a worker, do the action, pick up a different worker, do that action. You always end your turn holding exactly one worker. The worker you pick up becomes the one you place next turn. This constant cycling of workers is what makes the game flow.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: Hiring a Crew Member
walkthrough.text: They pick a townsfolk card with a Valkyrie icon — this crew member will die in a future raid and advance the Valkyrie track. Then Player two picks up a worker from another space and takes that action.

walkthrough.title: Player Three: Gathering Provisions
walkthrough.text: Then they pick up a worker from another village space. Provisions are the most common resource needed for raids, so stocking up early is smart.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Hiring Crew
walkthrough.text: Player one now holds the black worker. They place it on a village space and take that action, then pick up a white worker from another space. Player one uses this turn to hire a strong crew member with a military icon, paying silver. They are building toward a fortress raid later.

walkthrough.title: Player Three's Mistake: Hoarding Resources
walkthrough.text: Over the next few turns, Player three keeps gathering resources — silver, provisions, more provisions, iron. They have a stockpile of resources but have not hired any crew. Without crew, you cannot raid. Resources sitting in your supply do not score points. Player three has fallen into the hoarding trap.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Resources Do Not Score
walkthrough.text: Silver, provisions, and iron are all means to an end — they help you hire crew and execute raids. But they score zero points on their own. Only gold scores points directly. If you spend several turns hoarding without a plan to convert those resources into raids, you are falling behind players who are already raiding.

walkthrough.title: Player Two: First Raid
walkthrough.text: They place the black worker on a harbour space, discard the required crew cards and provisions, and collect their rewards: a plunder tile worth victory points, and offering tiles drawn from the bag. One of their discarded crew had a Valkyrie icon, so Player two advances on the Valkyrie track.

walkthrough.title: Why Early Raiding Pays Off
walkthrough.text: Player two just completed the first raid of the game. Harbours are cheap and easy, but they still provide plunder, offerings, and Valkyrie advancement. Early raids get you points on the board while other players are still preparing. Plus, the best offerings come from drawing first when the bag is full.

walkthrough.title: Player Three's Mistake: Ignoring the Valkyrie Track
walkthrough.text: Player three finally hires crew and raids a harbour, but they specifically avoided crew with Valkyrie icons because they did not want to lose their crew members. The problem is that the Valkyrie track is one of the highest-scoring elements in the game — up to sixteen points. By avoiding crew deaths, Player three is leaving a massive point source untapped.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Send Your Vikings to Valhalla
walkthrough.text: In Raiders of the North Sea, crew members dying in raids is not a setback — it is a feature. The Valkyrie track rewards you handsomely for sending Vikings to a glorious death. Deliberately hire cheap crew with Valkyrie icons and send them on raids knowing they will not return. The victory points from the Valkyrie track can easily decide the game.

walkthrough.title: Player One: Preparing for a Big Raid
walkthrough.text: Player one has been steadily hiring military crew and gathering iron. They are preparing for an outpost raid, which is harder than a harbour but gives better rewards. They place the black worker on an outpost, meet the crew and resource requirements, and collect a valuable plunder tile and multiple offering tiles.

walkthrough.title: Player Three's Mistake: Late Raiding
walkthrough.text: Player three is only now finishing their second raid while Players one and two are on their third. By waiting too long, Player three has fewer offerings to collect from the bag, less Valkyrie advancement, and fewer plunder tiles. The best raid locations and offerings are disappearing.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Do Not Wait Too Long to Raid
walkthrough.text: Raid timing is crucial. Every turn you spend in the village gathering resources is a turn you are not scoring points from raids. Start with cheap harbour raids as soon as you can afford them, then work your way up to harder targets. Waiting for the perfect moment means the best raid locations and offerings will already be claimed.

walkthrough.title: Continue Playing
walkthrough.text: Play continues with each player taking turns. The place-one-take-one rhythm stays the same throughout the entire game. Keep cycling between the village for resources and crew, then out to raid locations for points. Watch the endgame triggers — when offerings run out or enough Valkyrie tokens are claimed, the game is almost over.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You mastered the place-one-take-one rhythm, saw how to balance crew hiring with resource gathering, and learned the importance of raiding early. You watched Player three learn from three mistakes: hoarding resources, ignoring the Valkyrie track, and waiting too long to raid.

walkthrough.title: Before You Sail
walkthrough.text:
Before you start a full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics like raid timing and offering diversity.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to village actions, raid requirements, and scoring.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Hire Crew Early
walkthrough.text: Your first few turns should focus on hiring crew members. You cannot raid without crew, and raids are how you score. Spend your starting silver at the Barracks right away. Look for crew with Valkyrie icons — they are designed to die in raids and advance your Valkyrie track. A quick harbour raid with cheap crew is better than a perfectly planned fortress raid five turns from now.

walkthrough.title: Gather Provisions First
walkthrough.text: Provisions are the most universally needed resource for raids. Every raid tier requires them. Make gathering provisions a priority in your first few village turns. Iron is also important but mainly for outpost and fortress raids later. Livestock comes up less frequently. Provisions first, iron second, livestock as needed.

walkthrough.title: Target Harbours First
walkthrough.text: Harbour raids are the easiest and cheapest. They require fewer crew and fewer resources. Do not skip them thinking the rewards are too small — harbours still provide plunder tiles, offerings from the bag, and Valkyrie advancement. Early points compound over the game. Clear a harbour or two before moving to outposts and monasteries.

walkthrough.title: Embrace the Valkyrie Track
walkthrough.text: New players instinctively want to keep their crew alive. In this game, crew dying is intentional and rewarded. The Valkyrie track can score up to sixteen victory points — often the difference between winning and losing. Deliberately hire cheap crew with Valkyrie icons and send them on raids. Think of it as sending your Vikings to Valhalla for glory.

walkthrough.title: Diversify Your Offerings
walkthrough.text: Offerings score based on sets of different types. A set of five different offerings scores far more than five of the same type. When you have choices during raids, prioritize getting offering types you do not already have. The exponential scoring on diverse sets makes this one of the highest-value scoring categories in the game.

walkthrough.title: Plan Both Actions Together
walkthrough.text: Remember, you always perform two actions per turn — one from placing a worker and one from picking up a different worker. Plan both actions before you place your first worker. Think about which space you want to place on and which worker you want to pick up. Sometimes the pickup action is more valuable than the placement, so work backwards from what you need.

walkthrough.title: Do Not Hoard Resources
walkthrough.text: Resources sitting in your supply score zero points, with the exception of gold. Silver, provisions, iron, and livestock are tools to execute raids — convert them into raids as quickly as possible. If you have enough crew and resources for a raid, do the raid. Do not keep stockpiling for a perfect future raid that may never come.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Endgame Triggers
walkthrough.text: The game can end suddenly. Keep an eye on how many offering tiles remain in the bag, how many Valkyrie tokens have been claimed, and how many raid locations are completed. If the game is about to end, make sure you execute any remaining raids rather than gathering more resources. Unspent resources at game end are wasted opportunities.

bullets: You can't raid without crew — hire ASAP
Look for crew with Valkyrie icons
Quick harbour raid > perfectly planned fortress later

Stock up in early village turns

walkthrough.title: Crew Draft Optimization
walkthrough.text: The townsfolk row is your draft pool. Advanced players evaluate crew on three axes: cost efficiency (strength per silver), Valkyrie value (does this crew advance the track when they die), and special abilities. The ideal crew member is cheap, has a Valkyrie icon, and provides a useful ability. Do not overpay for crew with high strength but no Valkyrie icon — they are less efficient than two cheap Valkyrie crew.

walkthrough.title: Raid Sequencing
walkthrough.text: Plan your raid sequence across the entire game. Start with one or two harbour raids to get early points and offerings. Then move to outposts for better plunder. Save monasteries and fortresses for the mid to late game when you have the right crew and resources. Each raid tier requires specific crew types — military for fortresses, spiritual for monasteries. Build your crew roster to match your target sequence.

walkthrough.title: Worker Swap Manipulation
walkthrough.text: The place-one-take-one mechanic creates subtle strategic depth. By choosing which worker to pick up, you control which spaces are available to opponents. If you pick up a worker from a space an opponent wants, they cannot use it until someone places a worker there again. Think about denying key spaces to opponents when choosing your pickup action.

walkthrough.title: Valkyrie Track Racing
walkthrough.text: The Valkyrie track is competitive — higher positions score dramatically more points. If multiple players are advancing the track, it becomes a race. Plan your raids to include Valkyrie crew consistently. Falling behind on the track by even one or two positions can cost five or more points. Invest in cheap Valkyrie crew throughout the game, not just early on.

walkthrough.title: Offering Set Completion
walkthrough.text: Advanced offering strategy involves tracking which types you already have and prioritizing raids that give you missing types. When drawing from the bag, the randomness means you cannot guarantee specific types, but you can increase your draw count by targeting raids with more offering rewards. A complete set of different offerings can be worth more than an entire plunder tile.

walkthrough.title: Timing the Endgame
walkthrough.text: If you are ahead in points, accelerating the endgame is in your interest. Raid aggressively to deplete offerings and trigger the end before opponents can catch up. If you are behind, slow down — focus on high-value raids and Valkyrie advancement rather than clearing easy targets. Controlling the game's pace is a powerful advanced skill.

walkthrough.title: Gold vs. Raiding Allocation
walkthrough.text: Gold is worth one victory point each, and the Treasury is a safe action. But a single raid can generate five or more points through plunder, offerings, and Valkyrie combined. Advanced players use gold as a supplement, not a primary strategy. Visit the Treasury when other village actions are blocked or when you have a turn where no productive raid preparation is possible.

walkthrough.title: Fortress Timing
walkthrough.text: Fortress raids are the most rewarding but also the most expensive. They require large crews with military icons, significant iron, and often provisions. The mistake is either never attempting a fortress or preparing for one too early. The optimal timing is mid to late game — after you have cleared two or three easier raids and built up a military crew roster. One well-timed fortress raid can swing the entire game.

summary.title: Crew Draft Optimization
bullets: Evaluate: cost efficiency + Valkyrie value + special ability
Cheap Valkyrie crew often better than expensive strong crew
Two cheap crew > one expensive crew for raid flexibility

summary.title: Raid Sequencing
bullets: Harbours → Outposts → Monasteries/Fortresses
Match crew roster to target raid tier requirements
Military for fortresses, spiritual for monasteries

summary.title: Worker Swap Manipulation
bullets: Pick up workers from spaces opponents want
Control which spaces are available to opponents
Deny key spaces through strategic pickups

summary.title: Valkyrie Track Racing
bullets: Higher positions score dramatically more VP
Falling behind by 1-2 positions = 5+ VP cost
Include Valkyrie crew in raids consistently

summary.title: Offering Set Completion
bullets: Track which offering types you have vs. need
Target raids with more offering rewards
Complete set can be worth more than a plunder tile

summary.title: Timing the Endgame
bullets: Ahead: accelerate end through aggressive raiding
Behind: slow down, focus on high-value plays
Controlling game pace is a powerful skill

summary.title: Gold vs. Raiding Allocation
bullets: Gold = 1 VP each, safe but low ceiling
Single raid can generate 5+ VP (plunder + offerings + Valkyrie)
Use gold as supplement, not primary strategy

summary.title: Fortress Timing
bullets: Most rewarding but most expensive raids
Optimal timing: mid-to-late game after 2-3 easier raids
One well-timed fortress can swing the game

entries.definition: A player's personal worker piece used primarily on village spaces. Each player starts with one white worker. Used for the place step in village actions.
The single shared worker required to initiate raids. Starts on a designated village space. Must be placed on a raid location to raid. Cycles between village and raid locations.
The core turn mechanic. Place one worker on an empty space and perform its action, then pick up a different worker from an occupied space and perform that action. Always two actions per turn.
Village action space that grants silver tokens. Silver is the primary currency for hiring crew members.
Village action space that grants iron tokens. Iron is required for outpost and fortress raids.
Village action space that grants provision tokens. Provisions are required for all raid tiers and are the most universally needed resource.
Village action space where you hire crew members from the available row by paying their silver cost.
Village action space that grants gold tokens. Each gold token is worth one VP at end of game.
Village action space for recruiting townsfolk with special abilities. An alternative hiring location to the Barracks.
Village action space that lets you draw townsfolk cards from the deck. Provides more crew options for future hiring.
Village action space that provides provisions and helps prepare for upcoming raids.
The easiest raid tier. Requires fewer crew and resources. Provides plunder tiles and offering draws. Good targets for early raids.
Medium difficulty raid tier. Requires more crew and resources than harbours, including iron. Better rewards than harbours.
Hard raid tier. Requires crew with spiritual icons (cross) and significant provisions. Provides strong rewards.
The hardest raid tier. Requires the largest crews with military icons (sword), iron, and often provisions. Provides the best rewards in the game.
A crew member card showing hiring cost (silver), crew strength, special ability, and possibly Valkyrie or type icons. Hired from the available row and discarded to meet raid requirements.
Crew members with a sword icon. Required specifically for fortress raids. Provide combat strength for harder targets.
Crew members with a cross icon. Required specifically for monastery raids. Represent religious or spiritual warriors.
A symbol on some crew cards indicating the crew member dies during raids. When discarded in a raid, advance on the Valkyrie track for VP.
Currency token used to hire crew members from the available row. Does not score VP directly. Tiebreaker resource at game end.
Resource token worth one VP each at game end. Gained from Treasury actions and some raid rewards.
Resource token required for outpost and fortress raids. Gained from the Armoury village action.
Resource token required for all raid tiers. The most universally needed raid resource. Gained from the Provisions village action.
Resource token required for certain specific raids. Less common than provisions and iron.
Victory point tile earned from completing a raid. Each tile has a VP value revealed when collected. Primary point reward from raiding.
A scoring track on the board. Advance when crew with Valkyrie icons die in raids. Higher positions score more VP at game end, up to approximately sixteen points.
Tiles drawn from a cloth bag after raids. Come in different types. Score VP based on sets of different types — larger diverse sets score exponentially more.
Icons found on some plunder tiles and crew cards. Total armour icons collected score VP at game end.
Game ends when all offerings are taken, enough Valkyrie tokens are claimed, or enough raid locations are completed. Finish the current round for equal turns, then score.
Sum all VP: plunder tiles + Valkyrie track + offering sets + gold + armour + crew bonuses. Most VP wins. Tiebreaker: most remaining silver.
